content:
# Matthew Chapter 22 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_022_page_001.jpg, MAT_022_page_002.jpg

### The Parable of the Wedding Banquet

1. Jesus told them more parables.

2. "The kingdom of heaven is like a king who prepared a wedding banquet for his son.

3. He sent his servants to tell the invited guests that the feast was ready, but they refused to come.

4. He sent more servants with this message: 'Everything is prepared! The oxen and fattened cattle have been butchered, and the banquet is ready. Come to the celebration!'

5. But the guests couldn't be bothered. One went off to his farm, another to his business.

6. The rest grabbed the servants, beat them up, and killed them.

7. The king was furious. He sent his army, executed those murderers, and burned their city to the ground.

8. Then he told his servants, 'The banquet is ready, but the people I invited didn't deserve it.

9. Go to the street corners and invite everyone you can find.'

10. The servants went out into the streets and brought in everyone they could find — the bad along with the good — and the banquet hall was packed.

11. But when the king came in to meet the guests, he noticed a man who wasn't wearing wedding clothes.

12. 'Friend,' he said, 'how did you get in here without wedding clothes?' The man had nothing to say.

13. Then the king told the attendants, 'Tie him up hand and foot, and throw him into the darkness outside, where there will be weeping and grinding of teeth.'

14. Many are invited, but few are chosen."

### Paying Taxes to Caesar

15. Then the Pharisees went and hatched a plan to trap Jesus with his own words.

16. They sent their followers, along with supporters of Herod, to say, "Teacher, we know you're honest and teach God's way truthfully. You don't play favorites or try to please anyone.

17. So tell us what you think — is it right to pay taxes to Caesar or not?"

18. But Jesus saw through their scheme. "You hypocrites!" he said. "Why are you trying to trap me?

19. Show me the coin used to pay the tax." They handed him a Roman coin.

20. "Whose portrait is this?" he asked. "And whose inscription?"

21. "Caesar's," they replied. "Then give to Caesar what belongs to Caesar," he said, "and give to God what belongs to God."

22. They were stunned by his answer and walked away.

### Marriage and the Resurrection

23. That same day, some Sadducees — who say there is no resurrection — came with a question.

24. "Teacher," they said, "Moses told us that if a man dies without children, his brother must marry the widow and have children for him.

25. Now, there were seven brothers. The first married and died childless, leaving his wife to his brother.

26. The same thing happened with the second brother, the third, and so on through all seven.

27. Finally, the woman herself died.

28. So in the resurrection, whose wife will she be? All seven were married to her."

29. Jesus replied, "You're completely wrong — because you don't know the Scriptures and you don't know the power of God.

30. In the resurrection, people won't marry. They'll be like the angels in heaven.

31. But regarding whether the dead are raised — haven't you read what God himself said to you:

32. 'I am the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob'? He's not the God of dead people — he's the God of the living!"

33. The crowds were amazed at his teaching.

### The Greatest Commandment

34. When the Pharisees heard that Jesus had silenced the Sadducees, they got together.

35. One of them, an expert in the law, tested him with a question:

36. "Teacher, which is the most important commandment in the Law?"

37. Jesus answered, "'Love the Lord your God with all your heart, all your soul, and all your mind.'

38. This is the first and greatest commandment.

39. The second is equally important: 'Love your neighbor as yourself.'

40. The entire Law and all the Prophets are built on these two commandments."

### Whose Son Is the Messiah?

41. While the Pharisees were still gathered, Jesus asked them a question:

42. "What do you think about the Messiah? Whose son is he?" "David's son," they answered.

43. Jesus replied, "Then how do you explain that David, speaking under the inspiration of the Spirit, calls the Messiah 'Lord'? David said,

44. 'The Lord said to my Lord, "Sit at my right hand until I put your enemies under your feet."'

45. If David calls the Messiah 'Lord,' how can the Messiah be merely David's son?"

46. Nobody could answer him. And from that day on, no one dared ask him any more questions.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verses 1-14 — The wedding banquet:** This is the most violent of Jesus' parables. The king's response — sending armies and burning the city (v.7) — has been widely interpreted as a reference to the Roman destruction of Jerusalem in 70 CE. The twist at the end (the man without a wedding garment) warns that merely showing up is not enough. The invitation is free, but transformation is expected.

**Verse 21 — "Give to God what belongs to God":** This is not a neat separation of sacred and secular. The coin bears Caesar's image — so it belongs to Caesar. But human beings bear God's image (Genesis 1:27) — so they belong to God. If Caesar gets his coins, God gets... everything.

**Verse 30 — "like angels":** Jesus is not saying we become angels. He's saying that in the resurrection, the institution of marriage as we know it will not exist. The Sadducees' puzzle assumes that the next life is just an extension of this one. Jesus says the resurrection transforms the very categories they're arguing about.

**Verses 37-40 — The two greatest commandments:** Jesus combines Deuteronomy 6:5 (love God) and Leviticus 19:18 (love your neighbor). No rabbi before him had unified these two commands as the foundation of everything. "All the Law and the Prophets hang on these" — every rule, every prophecy, every story in the Hebrew Scriptures is an expression of these two loves.

**Verses 41-46 — David's Lord:** Jesus' counter-question is the last word in the debate series. If the Messiah is merely a descendant of David, why does David himself call him "Lord"? The implication: the Messiah transcends human categories. He is David's son — and David's Lord.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
